chunk_summary: This section focuses on the indications for Vitamin A supplementation in the management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM), emphasizing infection control and metabolic corrections based on established guidelines from WHO, Indian Academy of Pediatrics, and national health programs.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of Vitamin A supplementation in managing severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Identify specific indications and dosage guidelines for Vitamin A in SAM cases.
Learn the connection between Vitamin A supplementation and infection management in SAM.
Explore evidence-based practices for metabolic corrections in SAM patients.
key_facts: Vitamin A supplementation is critical for children with SAM to prevent blindness and reduce mortality.
WHO recommends a high-dose Vitamin A regimen: 50,000 IU for infants <6 months, 100,000 IU for 6–12 months, and 200,000 IU for children >12 months.
Vitamin A is administered on Day 1, Day 2, and Day 14 during SAM management.
Deficiency of Vitamin A increases susceptibility to infections and worsens malnutrition outcomes.
Guidelines emphasize monitoring for signs of Vitamin A toxicity, especially in repeated doses.
reference_quote: WHO guidelines recommend Vitamin A supplementation as a critical intervention for children with severe acute malnutrition to reduce mortality and prevent complications such as blindness and infections. (Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK154454/)
